coredata.dc:title: Morphological analysis of chromium in carbon quantum dots pairs Co-doped with zirconium and nitrogen and their applications in imaging of living cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ziting
Fan, Zhefeng
coredata.dc:description: As a new nanomaterial in the biochemistry field, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have been widely applied by scientists. In this study, CDs co-doped with zirconium and nitrogen (Zr-N-CDs) were synthesized quickly with lemon, ethylenediamine, and zirconium chloride through a hydrothermal method. The yield of Zr-N-CDs reached as high as 82.7%. The Zr-N-CDs showed outstanding water solubility in aqueous solution. The formation of Zr-N-CDs was verified by characterization technologies, such as high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). Moreover, the optical properties of Zr-N-CDs were investigated through fluorophotometer and ultraviolet spectroscopy. The synthesized Zr-N-CDs were applied to test hexavalent chromium (Cr (VI)), which showed a good linear relationship with the fluorescence quenching of Zr-N-CDs. The limit of detection was 0.52 µM. An analytical method for Cr morphology in natural water areas was developed in this experiment. The sensor showed good stability. The results demonstrate that the sensor detected 98.35%–100.9% Cr (VI) recovery rate in water samples. Based on the cytotoxicity of Zr-N-CDs to human cervical cancer cells (HeLa cells), the Zr-N-CDs had no evident cytotoxicity. The applications of Zr-N-CDs in bioimaging of cells were determined through laser scanning confocal microscopy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zr-N-CDs
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Speciation analysis of chromium
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: FL intensity quenching effect of Cr(VI) on Zr-N-CDs
Selectivity and interferences of Cr(VI) detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM images and size distribution histogram of Zr-N-CDs; b) HRTEM images of Zr-N-CDs; c) AFM image of Zr-N-CDs; d) XRD pattern of Zr-N-CDs.

a) FL intensity of Zr-N-CDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths; b) infrared spectrum of Zr-N-CDs and CDs; c) UV–Vis spectra of Zr-N-CDs (black), Cr(VI) (red), CDs (blue) and excitation and emission spectra of Zr-N-CDs (black); d) XPS spectra of Zr-N-CDs.
a) Zeta potential of Zr-N-CDs and CDs; b) excited-state lifetime images of Zr-N-CDs and Zr-N-CDs/Cr(VI).
Effects of ion strength on FL intensity of Zr-N-CDs.
a) Effect of different pH on the FL system; b) effect of reaction time on the FL system.
a) FL spectrum of Zr-N-CDs after adding different concentrations of Cr(VI); b) linear relative correlation between Cr(VI) concentration and FL intensity.
Selectivity and interference of Zr-N-CDs toward Cr(VI).
FL responses of Zr-N-CDs before and after transformation of Cr(III) into Cr(VI).
Survival rates of HeLa cells after processing by different concentrations of Zr-N-CDs.
FL images of Zr-N-CDs, Zr-N-CDs/Cr(III), and Zr-N-CDs/Cr(VI) in HeLa cells.
Comparison of different detection methods of Cr(VI).
Analysis of Cr forms in different water samples.
Recovery rate of Cr(VI) from water samples and analysis results.

head.ce:title: Morphological analysis of chromium in carbon quantum dots pairs Co-doped with zirconium and nitrogen and their applications in imaging of living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new nanomaterial in the biochemistry field, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have been widely applied by scientists. In this study, CDs co-doped with zirconium and nitrogen (Zr-N-CDs) were synthesized quickly with lemon, ethylenediamine, and zirconium chloride through a hydrothermal method. The yield of Zr-N-CDs reached as high as 82.7%. The Zr-N-CDs showed outstanding water solubility in aqueous solution. The formation of Zr-N-CDs was verified by characterization technologies, such as high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). Moreover, the optical properties of Zr-N-CDs were investigated through fluorophotometer and ultraviolet spectroscopy. The synthesized Zr-N-CDs were applied to test hexavalent chromium (Cr (VI)), which showed a good linear relationship with the fluorescence quenching of Zr-N-CDs. The limit of detection was 0.52 µM. An analytical method for Cr morphology in natural water areas was developed in this experiment. The sensor showed good stability. The results demonstrate that the sensor detected 98.35%–100.9% Cr (VI) recovery rate in water samples. Based on the cytotoxicity of Zr-N-CDs to human cervical cancer cells (HeLa cells), the Zr-N-CDs had no evident cytotoxicity. The applications of Zr-N-CDs in bioimaging of cells were determined through laser scanning confocal microscopy.

ce:section.ce:para: In the 1980s, Florence [1] predicted that scientists, including chemists and biochemists, may eventually shift their attention to morphological analysis. Many scientists have realized the significance of morphological analysis [2]. Increasing amounts of evidence reflect that chemical forms determine the bioactivity and toxicity of substances in the environment and influence their migration and transformation in living bodies and other environments. Focusing on the total amount of pollutants is inadequate to elaborate their ecological effects on the environment and accurately assess their environmental quality. Thus, studies should focus on the implementation of a specific morphological analysis.

The common forms of chromium include Cr(III) and Cr(VI). Given their different valence states, Cr(III) and Cr(VI) show significant variations in geochemical and biochemical properties and toxicity level. With the economic development and rapid industrial expansion, heavy metal pollution caused by industrial production is intensifying gradually, and environmental governance is becoming an essential step in the sustainable development of countries. Cr(VI) has caught the attention of scientists and environmentalists [3,4]. Cr(VI) exists as an anion and has relatively high activity and solubility. Cr(VI) quickly causes damages to plants and animals. If absorbed by the human body, Cr(VI) may threaten the kidney and myocardium. Ionic Cr(VI) is viewed as a carcinogenic upon contact with skin, accompanied with an allergic reaction. Cr(VI) can trigger adverse allergic responses, genetic defects, and cancers, and cells of human tissues and organs can extremely easily absorb Cr(VI). Moreover, Cr(VI) exhibits a high migration capability in water medium and mutagenesis, thus causing malformation [5]. Cr(VI) belongs to cancerogenic substances [6]. The industrial release of Cr(VI) is universal, and the annual emission of wastewater containing Cr(VI) reaches about 17,000 tons [7]. Therefore, Cr(VI) pollution migrates with the ecosystem, intensifying pollution and causing irreversible damages to the environment and human body. According to China’s standards of security, the practical allowable concentration of Cr(VI) is 2 mg/kg [8,9]. Meanwhile, Cr(III) is one of the essential microelements of the human body; it has weak activity and extremely low absorption rate by animals and plants. In the human body, Cr(III) complexes with globulin and is essential for normal globulin metabolism. As a result, Cr(VI) in the environment must be detected. Current Cr(VI) detection methods include atomic absorption spectrometry, ion chromatography, inductively coupled mass spectrometer, ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometry, and electrochemical techniques [9–12]. However, these technologies not only require abundant human resources and materials but also have the disadvantages of considerable time consumption, expensive devices, and complicated technologies. Hence, an effective, fast, and economical method must be selected to detect Cr(VI) and perform morphological analysis of Cr.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are a new type of spherical fluorescent nanomaterials with a diameter smaller than 10 nm. They exhibit excellent optical properties, facile functionalization, fast preparation, good water solubility, very low toxicity, easy surface modification, and high chemical stability [13,14]. CDs have been widely applied in various fields and show promising application prospects, including biosensing [15–17], drug delivery [18], and bioimaging [19,20]. Scientists have achieved the rapid development of CDs preparation technology, but they are still searching for CDs preparation methods with high yield and quality. To date, many scientists are doping CDs with N [21,22], S [21], P [23], Eu [24,25], and La [25] to improve their optical properties and electronic performances. Compared with many non-metal heteroatom adulterations, most metal ions, especially transition metal ions, easily lose electrons and have numerous vacant orbitals, resulting in a larger atomic radius compared with that of non-metal atoms. With the doping of metal ions, the excellent electron mobility of CDs will change the form and density of charge transformation between metal ions and the graphene matrix, thereby greatly changing the physical and chemical properties of CDs. Doped CDs mainly include copper-doped(Cu-CDs) [26,27], zinc-doped(Zn-CDs) [28], manganese-doped (Mn-CDs) [29], and cobalt-doped CDs (Co-CDs) [30]. These CDs are used for the detection of Fe3+, hydrogen peroxide, and glucose and the biological imaging and detection of Cr(VI). Magnesium and nitrogen co-doped CDs (Mg, N-CDs) [31,32]are used for cysteine ​​detection and biological imaging; iron and nitrogen co-doped CDs(Fe, N-CDs) are used for hydrogen peroxide detection. Other CDs include those doped with Ce [33], Ge [34], Tb [35], Eu [36], and Ru [37] and bimetallic Fe, Cu-doped CDs [38]. To our knowledge, no study has reported the doping of CDs with zirconium or zirconium co-doped with nitrogen to change the properties of CDs for utilization in biosensing and other fields.

In this study, we used citric acid as the carbon source and one-step hydrothermal method to quickly synthesize undoped CDs. To improve the properties of CDs, we developed a new type of zirconium and nitrogen co-doped CDs (Zr-N-CDs) using citric acid as the carbon source and ethylenediamine and zirconium chloride as the doping material. The Zr-N-CDs were applied to detect Cr(VI) based on fluorescence (FL) resonance energy transfer (FRET). The sensitivity and selectivity tests of Cr(VI) were performed through abundant FL quenching. Morphological analysis of Cr was carried out using the prepared Zr-N-CDs. Cr(III) was transformed into Cr(VI) by hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and the total Cr content was tested. The Cr(III) content was determined by the total Cr content minus the Cr(VI) content. The Zr-N-CDs were designed as FL probe to detect Cr(VI) in water areas and analyze the forms of Cr (Fig. 1 ).

The following reagents were bought from Alighting Chemical Co., LTD (Shanghai, China): citric acid, ethylenediamine, zirconium chloride, potassium dichromate, and all ionic liquids. Tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (C4H11NO3), quinine sulfate, hydrochloric acid (HCl), and other reagents were bought from Tianjin Photochemical Reagent Co., LTD. Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM), fetal serum, 0.25% pancreatin, and Cell Counting Kit-8 reagents were purchased from Shanghai Bioengineering Co., LTD. All chemical drugs were analytically pure and can be used directly without further purification. Germany Millipore ultrapure water purification system was used to prepare the ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) used in the experiments.

The synthesis of CDs was performed through following procedure: dissolution of citric acid in 40 ml ultrapure water and then the addition of 1 ml ethylenediamine. The synthesis of Zr-N-CDs was achieved by dissolving 2 g citric acid and 1 g zirconium chloride in 40 ml ultrapure water, followed by the addition of 1 ml ethylenediamine. After the above two mixtures were completely dissolved in water, they were transferred to a lined autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 2 h. The subsequent treatment was carried out under the same conditions. After the mixture was cooled to room temperature, it was centrifuged for 15 min at the rate of 10,000 r/min, and the supernatant was collected and further filtered by a 0.22 mm filtering device to obtain a uniformly dispersed brown solution. The solution was transferred to an interception bag with an interception quantity of 3000 Da to ensure the uniform particle size and high yield rate of the gained fluorescent CDs. The interception bag was purified for 24 h in ultrapure water at room temperature. Finally, the solution was frozen for 12 h in the refrigerator, and the samples were dried by a freezer dryer, yielding solids with good water solubility.

First, 0.20 g L-1 Zr-N-CDs standard solution was prepared by using ultrapure water for follow-up experiment. The whole experiment was carried out under optimized conditions. To detect Cr(VI), we added 10 µL Zr-N-CDs solution, 250 µL phosphate-bufferedsaline (PBS) (0.2 M, pH = 7.0), and a series of different concentrations of Cr(VI) solution to a 10 ml colorimetric tube and finally dissolved the mixture to a constant volume of 5 ml. After the uniform reaction of the mixture at room temperature, the FL spectrum of the excited Zr-N-CDs at 350 nm was collected. The slit widths of excitation and emission were 5 and 20 nm, respectively.

In morphological analysis, the mixture of Cr(VI) and Cr(III) was added to the Zr-N-CDs solution to record the changes in the FL spectrum with the assumption that Cr(III) causes no effect on the FL of Zr-N-CDs. Next, the total Cr content was tested, and 30% hydrogen peroxide was used to oxidize Cr(III) in the mixture into Cr(VI) completely. Later, residual hydrogen peroxides were eliminated through thermal decomposition. Then, we analyzed Cr(VI) in accordance with a previously described process after being cooled to room temperature. Finally, the Cr(III) concentration was determined as the total Cr concentration minus Cr(VI) concentration.

The toxicity of Zr-N-CDs to HeLa cells was tested by 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazole-2-group)-2,5-diphenyl l-2-H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. Normal HeLa cells were added to a 96-hole plate and incubated for 24 h in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 ℃. Full culture solution (90% DMEM + 10% fetal serum) was used to prepare different concentrations (0, 10, 20, 30, and 40 µL) of Zr-N-CDs, which were continuously incubated for 24 h in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 ℃. Subsequently, the reaction products were rinsed with PBS (pH = 7.4). Meanwhile, unprocessed normal cells were used as the control group, and the absorbance value of testing pores was read by a microplate reader. Cell proliferation rate was calculated using Eq. (1), where A is the absorbance value after cell processing by the probe, and A0 is the absorbance value of cells unprocessed by probes.
(1)
V R =
A
A 0
× 100 %

After the cells were cultured, 10 µL Zr-N-CDs solution was added to the culture media with cells. After incubation for 2 h at 37 ℃, the cells were rinsed thrice by PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4). FL imaging of cells was performed under a laser scanning confocal microscope. Then, 70 µM Cr(III) and Cr(VI) ion solution was added to the above culture media, and incubation was continued for 2 h. Subsequently, the FL images of cells were acquired.

The CDs and Zr-N-CDs synthesized by the one-step hydrothermal method exhibited excellent water solubility. According to the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. S1a), the CDs had good dispersibility. The average particle size of the CDs (1.07 nm, Fig. S1b) was obtained through dynamic light scattering. Zr-N-CDs doped showed good dispersibility in the TEM images, with an average particle diameter size of 3.35 nm (Fig. 2 a). Given the doping of zirconium and nitrogen, the particle size increased significantly. The two types of CDs were almost spherical and displayed a uniformed size with good dispersion and no evident aggregation phenomenon. Under high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), The Zr-N-CDs presented visible lattice strips (Fig. 2b). [39]Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images showed that the height of Zr-N-CDs was about 1.5 nm (Fig. 2c). Next, the crystal structure of Zr-N-CDs was analyzed through X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. 2d), in which the broad peak at 23° corresponded to the lattice spacing of carbon-based material (002) [40]; however, no other characteristic peak was observed. Moreover, abundant defects caused by functional groups might have been present, and they can be attributed to the long-term stability and inevident clustering of Zr-N-CDs.[41] Under UV light irradiation, CDs and Zr-N-CDs all emitted blue light, indicating their photoluminescence (PL). When CDs were excited at 340 nm, the maximum emission was concentrated at 440 nm (Fig. S2). When Zr-N-CDs were excited at 350 nm, the largest emission was concentrated at 450 nm (Fig. 3 a). With the doping of zirconium and nitrogen, the emission peak position shifted to the red area.

According to the characterization results of the main functional groups of CDs and Zr-N-CDs by Fourier-transform infrared spectrophotometry (Fig. 3b), the tensile vibrations at 3419, 1374, and 1056 cm−1 indicate that the CDs contained rich functional groups, such as O-H/N-H, CH2, and C-O-C, respectively. The tensile vibrations at 3419, 3087, 1556, and 1056 cm−1 suggest that the doping of zirconium and nitrogen provided Zr-N-CDs with functional groups rich in O-H, N-H, C = O, and C-O-C, respectively. Water solubility of quantum dots was altered by the presence of functional groups containing nitrogen and oxygen on their surface. Fig. 3c shows the UV absorption spectra of our prepared CDs and Zr-N-CDs. The CDs and Zr-N-CDs presented a main absorption peak at 349 nm, which might have been caused by the n-π* electronic transition [42,43]. The weak peak of CDs and Zr-N-CDs at 241 nm was caused by the π-π* transitions in the carbonyl and amide bonds (C = C/C = O). Fig. 3d shows the morphological structure analysis of Zr-N-CDs by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The C1s (288 eV), O1s (532 eV), N1s (402 eV), and Zr3d (185 eV) peaks indicated the presence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and zirconium, respectively, consistent with the results of elemental analysis (Fig. S3). The zeta potentials of Zr-N-CDs and CDs (both of which had negative zeta potentials) in water were −1.09 and −3.16, respectively (Fig. 4 a). The carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface can cause a series of emission traps, which are important factors for achieving a strong PL performance.

The quantum yields (QYs) and service lives of Zr-N-CDs and CDs were further studied. The QY of quinine sulfate [44] in 0.10 M H2SO4 solution (QY = 0.56) was used as FL standard. QY can be calculated using the following equation:
(2)
ɸ
x
=
ɸ
st
I x
/
I
st
η
x
2
/
η
st
2
A
st
/
A x

The QY of Zr-N-CDs and CDs was calculated. In the above calculation formula,
ɸ
x
is QY, I is the integral area of FL emission strength at 350 nm excitation and 450 nm emission wavelengths, Ƞ is the refractive index of reagent, and A is the UV absorption strength of Zr-N-CDs at 350 nm. st and x refer to the standard and testing samples, respectively. Given the doping of Zr and N, QY increased from 14% (Table S1) to 82.7% (Table S2). The lifetime of Zr-N-CDs was about 11.73 ns (Fig. 4b), and the lifetime of CDs was about 6.07 ns (Fig. S4). The results show that the FL lifetime of QDs increased significantly with the doping of Zr and N.

To evaluate the FL stability of Zr-N-CDs and the potential for subsequent biological applications, we recorded the effect of sodium chloride solution (NaCl) on the FL intensity of Zr-N-CDs at different concentrations: 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1 M. As shown in Fig. 5 , the FL intensity of the quantum dots remained the same, suggesting their good FL stability.

Different pH had varied effects on the quenching of Zr-N-CDs based on Cr(VI) solution. Therefore, pH must be optimized. Fig. 6 a shows the FL quenching efficiency (Effq) of Zr-N-CDs by Cr(VI) at PBS concentration of 0.2 M and pH 4.0–9.0.

The calculation formula of quenching efficiency is as follows:
(3)
E f f q
( % )
=
(
F 0
- F )
/
F 0
where F and F0 are the FL intensities at 450 nm in the Zr-N-CDs solution with and without Cr(VI), respectively. Fig. 6a shows that the Effq of Zr-N-CDs generally increased when pH was between 4.0 and 7.0 and reached the maximum at pH = 7.0. However, the Effq of Zr-N-CDs declined gradually as the pH continually increased. After careful consideration, we considered pH = 7.0 as the best value for FL quenching.
The influence of time (1, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, and 40 min) on Zr-N-CDs FL quenching effect of Cr(VI) in PBS with pH 7.0 was recorded (Fig. 6b). Time showed no influence on the FL quenching effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL intensity quenching effect of Cr(VI) on Zr-N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 a reveals that when the pH of PBS was 7.0, the FL intensity of CDs declined gradually with the addition of different concentrations of Cr(VI) solution to Zr-N-CDs (10 µL, 0.20 g L-1) solution. When the Cr(VI) concentration reached 100 µM, the FL intensity of Zr-N-CDs decreased by 65%, and the FL spectrum was collected at the 350 nm excitation wavelength. Meanwhile, the linear relationship between Cr(VI) concentration and FL intensity of Zr-N-CDs was set up (Fig. 7b). Within the concentration range of 0.4–100 µM, the Effq of Zr-N-CDs showed a good linear relationship with Cr(VI) concentration. The linear equation is F/F0 = -0.0064x + 0.9733 (R2 = 0.9955), where F0 and F are the FL intensities of Zr-N-CDs without and with Cr(VI), respectively.

Table 1 shows the recent recognition methods, linear range, and limit of detection (LOD) of Cr(VI) compared with this study. In past reports, although FL detection techniques (dual emissive CDs) have relatively high sensitivity, the LODs of 0.4 µM were lower than the values obtained in this study ((3σ/κ) 0.52 µM). However, the proposed Zr-N-CDs are easy to synthesize and have high QY and low economic costs. Thus, Zr-N-CDs not only have simple synthesis, low economic cost, and are environment-friendly but also have QY as high as 82.7% due to co-doping with Zr and N.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interferences of Cr(VI) detection
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the selective detection of Cr(VI) by zirconium and nitrogen co-CDs, we selected 100 µM hexavalent chromium, 10 mM different ions(Na+, Cl-, Co2+, Cu2+, S2−, Zn2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Ca2+, K+, Cr3+, Br-, OH−, ClO−, CO3 2−, Fe3+, Fe2+, F-, HCO3 –, HPO4 2-, H2PO4-, and PO4 3-), and intracellular compounds (triphosphate, glutathione, tryptophan, leucine, isoleucine, methionine, and glucose) to add to the Zr-N-CDs solution to verify the selectivity and anti-disturbance capability of Zr-N-CDs for Cr(VI) detection. When silver ions were present in the system, silver ions and chromate formed silver chromate precipitates, which interfered with the detection of Cr(VI). Therefore, we used a masking method that uses sodium chloride (NaCl) as a masking agent. A 20 mM NaCl solution (NaCl/Ag+: 2/1) was added to the system to form a silver chloride precipitate with silver ions to further prevent the complexation of silver ions with chromate, thereby eliminating the interference of silver ions for the detection of Cr(VI). The black bars in Fig. 8 indicate the FL quenching efficiency of Zr-N-CDs after the addition of different substances. Compared with Cr(VI), 24 kinds of ions, such as Na+, and 7 kinds of intracellular compounds, such as triphosphate, had no effect on the FL intensity of the quantum dots. Thus, Zr-N-CDs have a good specificity for the detection of Cr(VI). The interference of 24 kinds of ions, such as Na+, and 7 kinds of intracellular compounds, such as triphosphate, on Cr(VI) detection was also evaluated. As indicated by the red bar in Fig. 8, when potential ions and intracellular compounds co-existed with Cr(VI), the FL intensity of the Zr-N-CDs/Cr(VI) system underwent no remarkable change, suggesting that this sensor system has remarkable anti-interference capability.

To better understand the quenching mechanism of Zr-N-CDs, we determined the absorption peak of Cr(VI) overlapping with the excitation and emission peaks of Zr-N-CDs (Fig. 3c). Therefore, the quenching mechanism of Zr-N-CDs may be caused by FRET [50]or internal filtration effect (IFE)[51,52]. Changes in the FL lifetime of quantum dots and calculation of the distance between Zr-N-CDs and Cr(VI) can be used as an effective method to distinguish FRET or IFE [53]. Fig. 4b shows the FL lifetime in the absence and presence of Cr(VI). In the presence of Cr(VI), the lifetime of quantum dots reduced from the initial value of 11.73 ns to 6.76 ns. According to the Forster energy transfer theory, the relationship between energy transfer efficiency E and the distance r between the donor–acceptor and critical energy transfer distance R0 is computed as follows [54]:
(4)
E = 1 -
F
F 0
=
R
0
6
R
0
6
+
r
6
where F is the FL intensity of Zr-N-CDs in the presence of Cr(VI), F0 is the FL intensity of Zr-N-CDs without Cr(VI), and R0 is the critical distance when E = 50%.
(5)
R
0
6
= 8.79 ×
10
- 25
K
2
n
- 4
∅ J

According to the literature, k2 in Formula (5) represents the relative orientation of transition dipoles of Zr-N-CDs and Cr(VI) in space (k2 = 2/3 represents the random orientation in the fluid solution), and n is the refractive index of the medium (1.336) [55,56], and ∅ is the QY of Zr-N-CDs in the absence of an acceptor (82.7%). J is the overlapping integral of absorption spectrum between the FL spectrum of the donor (Zr-N-CDs) and acceptor (Cr(VI)):
(6)
J =
∑ F
( λ )
ε
( λ )
λ
4
Δ λ
∑ F ( λ ) Δ λ

In Formula 6, F(λ) is the FL intensity of the fluorescent donor quantum dot at wavelength λ, and ε(λ) is the molar absorption coefficient of the acceptor at wavelength λ; thus, J = 1.5 × 10-19 cm3 L mol−1. Using Formulas (4)–(6), E equaled 0.65, R0 was 0.53 nm, and r was 0.48 nm, which is considerably smaller than 10 nm. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of Zr-N-CDs was caused by FRET based on the above results.

Cr(VI) in reagent water samples was tested to verify the feasibility of the experiment. A certain amount of Cr(VI) was added to the reagent water samples by standard addition method. The experiment was repeated thrice, and the final results are listed in Table 3 . The recovery rate of marked samples was between 98.35% and 100.9%, and the reproducibility was 1.52%–2.89% (RSD%, n = 3). Results demonstrate that Zr-N-CDs achieved high accuracy and were hardly interfered by the matrix. Thus, Zr-N-CDs have promising application prospects in water sample detection.

The cytotoxicity of Zr-N-CDs to HeLa cells was studied through the MTT method to research and explore the potential applications of Zr-N-CDs in bioimaging and Cr imaging of cells (Fig. 10 ). Based on the proliferation rate of HeLa cells cultured in different concentrations (10, 20, 30, and 40 µL) of Zr-N-CDs, the synthesized Zr-N-CDs showed no evident toxicity. When the concentration was kept at 40 µL, the cell proliferation rate remained at about 85%, indicating the relatively low cytotoxicity of Zr-N-CDs. Moreover, bioimaging requires a considerably low Zr-N-CDs concentration in practical applications. Based on the above analysis, Zr-N-CDs are expected to be a potential application material for bioimaging.

Given their relations with cytotoxicity, oxidative stress, and carcinogenicity, tracking heavy metals in biological environment and human body attracts wide attention.[57] Given their good stability, biocompatibility, very low cytotoxicity, uniform distribution, and small size, Zr-N-CDs have been applied in bioimaging experiment of human cervical cancer cells (HeLa cells). Fig. 11 shows that in the presence of Cr(III), the FL intensity of Zr-N-CDs in cells was not quenched. This result was caused by the difficult entry of Cr(III) into cells; its entrance will increase the cell membrane permeability. In the presence of Cr(VI), the FL of Zr-N-CDs was quenched in cells due to the FRET quenching effect. Thus, Zr-N-CDs are applicable to bioimaging of Cr(VI) in cells.

In this study, Zr-N-CDs were synthesized by using citric acid, ethylenediamine, and zirconium chloride. Zr-N-CDs showed high QY, excellent FL payable and stability, short preparation time, and environment-friendly characteristics. Given that FRET, which is generated by high overlapping between the UV absorption peak of Cr(VI) and excitation peak of Zr-N-CDs, interacts with Cr(VI), a simple, sensitive, and fast test method was proposed to analyze Cr forms in water samples and perform imaging of Cr(VI) in HeLa cells.

Ziting Zhang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing - original draft. Zhefeng Fan: Investigation, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.119248.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

J. Phys. Chem. A
